questions: Apple operates a localized online store for a Southeast Asian city-state where orders are accepted only from domestic billing addresses, products are shipped only within that country, and refunds are remitted solely to matching domestic bank accounts. For that specific regional store, explain: (1) the standard return window and required product condition; (2) any special notes that apply when returning a smartphone from Apple’s flagship mobile line; (3) the additional rules and fees for products purchased in volume; (4) the time frame and limitations for requesting a refund if the product’s price is reduced after purchase; and (5) how returns are handled for both prescription and non-prescription ZEISS optical inserts.

On Apple’s official macOS support resources, several core help sections are designed to orient new users, assist switchers from other platforms, explain cross-device integration, introduce major operating system updates, highlight privacy and security protections (including credential recovery), provide model-specific setup guidance, and describe Apple’s built-in AI system. Identify each of these distinct support sections and explain the primary purpose and key features emphasized in each one. Your response should clearly differentiate all sections and summarize the main capabilities or assistance they provide.

A Mac user wants to begin using the system’s built‑in personal AI capabilities across their Apple devices and ensure their computer is properly maintained for compatibility. Based on Apple’s official platform guidance, explain: (1) the hardware models and minimum operating system versions required across Mac, iPhone, iPad, Apple Watch, and Apple Vision Pro; (2) the setup steps and device conditions necessary to activate the AI features (including storage, language, pairing, and download requirements); (3) notable regional or language limitations that may affect availability; and (4) the recommended built‑in macOS support actions for maintaining system readiness, including password recovery, operating system reinstallation, backups, and system updates. Provide a structured explanation that integrates requirements and maintenance considerations across all relevant devices.

rounds.question: Apple operates a localized online store for a Southeast Asian city-state where orders are accepted only from domestic billing addresses, products are shipped only within that country, and refunds are remitted solely to matching domestic bank accounts. For that specific regional store, explain: (1) the standard return window and required product condition; (2) any special notes that apply when returning a smartphone from Apple’s flagship mobile line; (3) the additional rules and fees for products purchased in volume; (4) the time frame and limitations for requesting a refund if the product’s price is reduced after purchase; and (5) how returns are handled for both prescription and non-prescription ZEISS optical inserts.
checklist_width: Standard return policy: 14-day return window with receipt
Product must be in original condition with included parts, accessories, and packaging
Refund issued based on original payment method
iPhone return note: does not automatically cancel/reset wireless service; customer responsible for carrier agreement and fees
Volume purchases: more than 4 per order or across orders in same category
Volume return window: 7 days from receipt
Volume returns subject to 25% restocking fee per item
Volume returns must be returned to original store of purchase
Price protection: request within 14 calendar days of receiving product if price drops
Customer must contact Apple within 14 days of price change
Excludes limited-time promotions; limited to 10 units
Prescription ZEISS Optical Inserts: return within 14 days via order listing page or phone; not returnable to retail store location
ZEISS Readers (non-prescription): returnable within 14 days, including at retail store location
checklist_depth: Identification of the Apple Online Store Singapore (city-state in Southeast Asia; ships only within Singapore; refunds only to Singapore bank accounts)
Confirmation that policies described correspond specifically to this regional Singapore store’s terms and conditions
rounds.rationale: The question uses geographic and policy-based clues (a Southeast Asian city-state store with domestic-only shipping and refund restrictions) to indirectly identify the Apple Online Store Singapore without naming it (Deep reasoning). It then requires aggregating multiple distinct policy sections—standard returns, iPhone-specific conditions, volume purchase rules, price protection terms, and ZEISS optical insert procedures—spread across the store’s terms and conditions (Wide coverage).
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Singapore Consumer Store Thanks for shopping at Apple. We appreciate the fact that you like to buy the cool stuff we build. We also want to make sure you have a rewarding expe
rounds.answer_a:
For Apple’s Singapore Online Store, standard items may be returned within **14 calendar days** after you receive them. Items must be returned with the original receipt, packaging, accessories, manuals, and any bundled promotional items; Apple expects them to be in original condition, though opened packaging is generally allowed if the product is undamaged.[1]

For an **iPhone**, Apple notes that the device should have **Find My iPhone/Activation Lock disabled** before return; otherwise the return may not be accepted or processing may be delayed. As with other products, all included items should be sent back.[1]

For **multiple-unit or volume purchases**, if you bought **more than ten of the same product**, Apple treats the order differently: returns are subject to a **15% open-box fee**, and such products may be returned only if they are unopened, unless local consumer law provides otherwise.[1]

If Apple reduces the price of a product after purchase, you may request a **refund or credit of the difference within 14 calendar days** of receiving the product. This applies only to products bought directly from Apple. It does **not** apply to limited-time price reductions such as sales events, including special promotional campaigns.[1]

For **ZEISS Optical Inserts** sold for Apple Vision Pro in Singapore, **non-prescription Reader inserts** may be returned under Apple’s normal return policy, but **prescription Optical Inserts are non-returnable and non-refundable**, except where required by law. Apple recommends checking prescription details carefully before ordering.[2][3]

## References
[1] Apple Singapore Online Store – Sales & Refund Terms and Conditions. https://www.apple.com/sg/shop/browse/open/salespolicies  
[2] Apple Singapore Online Store – Apple Vision Pro / ZEISS Optical Inserts shopping information. https://www.apple.com/sg/shop/buy-vision/apple-vision-pro  
[3] Apple Support / Apple Store policy information for ZEISS Optical Inserts (Singapore region). https://support.apple.com/en-sg/118484
rounds.answer_b:
Apple Store (Singapore) Policies  

1. Standard returns: Most hardware purchased from the Singapore Apple Online Store may be returned within 14 calendar days of delivery. Items must be in “as-new” condition, with all original packaging, accessories, manuals and proof-of-purchase; any bundled software that has been opened is non-returnable [1].  

2. iPhone specifics: Before sending back an iPhone, you must disable Find My iPhone and erase all personal data; if the device is locked to your Apple ID, Apple will refuse the return. SIM cards or eSIM profiles are not returned; service-plan charges remain your responsibility [2].  

3. Volume purchases: Orders of more than eight units of the same product (or more than ten iPhones) are treated as “Volume Sales.” Such items may be returned only if defective; voluntary change-of-mind returns are refused. A 25 % restocking fee applies to any approved exception, and shipping charges are non-refundable [1][3].  

4. Price-protection: If Apple lowers the price of an item within 14 days after you receive it, you may request a refund of the difference. The request must be submitted online or by phone within that 14-day window; price-adjustments are limited to one request per product and do not apply to clearance, limited-time promotions or refurbished goods [1].  

5. ZEISS optical inserts: Non-prescription ZEISS inserts for Vision Pro follow the standard 14-day return policy; they must be undamaged and in original packaging. Custom prescription ZEISS inserts are final sale and cannot be returned or refunded once the order has been confirmed, except where required by consumer-protection law [4].  

## References  
[1] Apple Store (Singapore) – Sales and Refund Policy.  
[2] Apple Singapore – iPhone Returns Section.  
[3] Apple Singapore – Quantity Limits & Restrictions.  
[4] Apple Singapore – Vision Pro ZEISS Optical Inserts Return Terms.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the Apple Online Store Singapore (the Southeast Asian city-state store with domestic-only billing, shipping, and refunds). So both pass the entity check.

Width/Completeness: Both fail multiple critical checklist items. Neither mentions the 7-day return window for volume purchases, nor the requirement that volume returns be sent back to the original store of purchase. Both incorrectly describe ZEISS prescription inserts as non-returnable, contradicting the 14-day return (via order page/phone, not retail store) requirement. Both also miss the key iPhone-specific note about wireless service agreements and carrier responsibility. Agent A further hallucinates a 15% restocking fee and a >10-unit threshold; Agent B hallucinates different quantity thresholds and defective-only restrictions (though it correctly states a 25% fee). Both omit the 10-unit limitation in price protection.

Presentation & UX: Both are clearly structured and readable with numbered sections. Agent A is slightly cleaner and more concise; Agent B includes marginally more operational detail (e.g., refund request channels). However, since both contain substantial factual inaccuracies across multiple required sub-points, neither delivers a reliably accurate search-agent experience.

Conclusion: Because both fail several core checklist requirements (WIDE errors) despite identifying the correct store, this is a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.question: On Apple’s official macOS support resources, several core help sections are designed to orient new users, assist switchers from other platforms, explain cross-device integration, introduce major operating system updates, highlight privacy and security protections (including credential recovery), provide model-specific setup guidance, and describe Apple’s built-in AI system. Identify each of these distinct support sections and explain the primary purpose and key features emphasized in each one. Your response should clearly differentiate all sections and summarize the main capabilities or assistance they provide.
checklist_width: Explains initial Mac setup: choosing wallpaper, changing common settings, starting apps, making the most of a new Mac
Describes transition guidance for users switching from Windows, including navigation help and file transfer from PC
Details Mac integration with other Apple devices: viewing/controlling iPhone from Mac, copy and paste between devices
Summarizes new features in macOS Tahoe: refreshed design, making phone calls from Mac, enhanced Spotlight actions, automatic translations in calls and messages
Outlines privacy and security tools: password management, private browsing, built-in protections
Mentions ability to recover a recently deleted password or passkey
References model-specific Getting Started guides for new Mac owners
Describes Apple Intelligence as personal, private, and integrated into macOS
checklist_depth: ‘Get started with your Mac’ section + focuses on initial setup and basic customization
‘Switched from Windows to Mac?’ section + specifically targets former Windows users and file migration
‘Use your Mac with other Apple devices’ section + emphasizes seamless ecosystem integration features
‘What’s new in macOS Tahoe’ section + introduces latest OS features and productivity/communication enhancements
‘Control your privacy and security’ section + centers on built-in protections and credential recovery
‘Get to know your Mac’ (model-specific Getting Started guide) + tied to particular Mac models
‘Apple Intelligence’ section + identified as integrated, private AI within macOS
rounds.rationale: The question masks the specific section titles by describing their functional roles (Deep reasoning), requiring the agent to infer the correct official macOS help sections. It then demands aggregation of key features from all identified sections (Wide scope), ensuring the answer compiles multiple distinct support topics rather than relying on a single source.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Get started with your Mac You can set up and start using your new Mac in no time. Choose a wallpaper for your desktop, change common settings, start using apps and more. Learn
rounds.answer_a:
Apple’s macOS support resources highlight several distinct onboarding and guidance sections. **macOS User Guide** is the broad starting point: it explains setup, navigation, apps, files, settings, accessibility, continuity features, and troubleshooting for everyday Mac use [1]. **Switch from Windows to Mac** is aimed at PC migrants, mapping familiar Windows tasks to macOS equivalents such as Finder, desktop organization, keyboard shortcuts, storage, and app installation, helping users adapt quickly [2].

**Continuity** focuses on cross-device integration across Apple products, covering features like Handoff, AirDrop, Universal Clipboard, Continuity Camera, Sidecar, and using iPhone calls/texts on a Mac, emphasizing seamless workflows between Mac, iPhone, iPad, and Apple Watch [3]. **What’s new in macOS Sequoia** introduces the latest operating-system capabilities, highlighting major updates such as iPhone Mirroring, window tiling, Safari improvements, passwords enhancements, and gaming or productivity additions [4].

**Privacy and Security** explains protections built into Mac, including secure sign-in, app permissions, encryption, browsing safeguards, software update security, and account/device protection [5]. Within that area, **If you forgot your Mac login password** provides credential-recovery help, including reset options using Apple ID, Recovery, or another admin account depending on configuration [6].

**Mac model-specific setup guides** offer tailored instructions for particular hardware families—such as MacBook Air, MacBook Pro, iMac, Mac mini, Mac Studio, and Mac Pro—covering physical setup, ports, accessories, migration, and first-run basics [7]. Finally, **Apple Intelligence on Mac** introduces Apple’s built-in AI system, emphasizing writing tools, image and language features, and privacy-aware on-device or Private Cloud Compute processing [8].

## References
[1] Apple Support, “macOS User Guide.” https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/welcome/mac  
[2] Apple Support, “Switch from Windows to Mac.” https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/switch-from-windows-to-mac-mchlbc684e49/mac  
[3] Apple Support, “Use Continuity to connect your Mac, iPhone, iPad, and Apple Watch.” https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/use-continuity-to-connect-your-mac-iphone-ipad-apple-watch-mchl1d734309/mac  
[4] Apple Support, “What’s new in macOS Sequoia.” https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/whats-new-in-macos-sequoia-mchl2ca4f8f3/mac  
[5] Apple Support, “Protect your Mac and information.” https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/protect-your-mac-and-information-mh11401/mac  
[6] Apple Support, “If you forgot your Mac login password.” https://support.apple.com/en-us/102633  
[7] Apple Support, “Mac User Guides.” https://support.apple.com/mac  
[8] Apple Support, “Get started with Apple Intelligence on Mac.” https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/get-started-with-apple-intelligence-mh35738/mac
rounds.answer_b:
Mac Basics – Orientation hub that walks new users through the desktop, Finder, Dock, System Settings, gestures, files and folders, and built-in apps so they can navigate macOS confidently [1].  

Switch to Mac – Dedicated help for Windows switchers; explains Migration Assistant, importing email/bookmarks, keyboard-shortcut differences, right-clicking, and installing familiar software, easing the changeover process [2].  

Use Mac with other devices (Continuity) – Shows how to link Mac, iPhone, iPad, Apple Watch and AirPods; features include Handoff, Universal Clipboard, AirDrop, Continuity Camera, Sidecar, Instant Hotspot, and iPhone calls/messages on Mac, enabling a seamless cross-device workflow [3].  

macOS Sonoma Support – Update-specific section covering how to check compatibility, back up and install, plus guides to new functions such as desktop widgets, Game Mode, Presenter Overlay, Safari profiles, and enhanced accessibility tools [4].  

Privacy & Security – Central portal highlighting FileVault encryption, Gatekeeper, sandboxing, Rapid Security Response, passkeys, App Tracking Transparency, and recovery options like Account Recovery contacts and Recovery Assistant for forgotten passwords or locked Apple IDs [5].  

Mac Essentials (model-specific) – Setup pages for each Mac model (e.g., MacBook Pro Essentials, Mac mini Essentials); include port diagrams, accessory pairing, power/battery or display tips, Apple silicon vs. Intel notes, and first-time configuration steps [6].  

Use Siri on Mac – Describes Apple’s on-device AI assistant; details activation methods, voice queries to open apps, search files, control HomeKit, set reminders, dictate text, and manage Siri data privacy or language settings [7].  

## References  
[1] https://support.apple.com/mac/mac-basics  
[2] https://support.apple.com/guide/migration-assistant/welcome/mac  
[3] https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT204681  
[4] https://support.apple.com/macos/sonoma  
[5] https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT201303  
[6] https://support.apple.com/mac  
[7] https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/use-siri-on-mac-mh15216/mac
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified most of the core support sections (new user orientation, Windows switchers, Continuity integration, privacy/security, and model-specific guides). However, Deep Logic differs on two key entities. Agent A correctly includes the dedicated “Apple Intelligence” section, describing it as integrated and privacy-focused AI within macOS. Agent B instead substitutes “Use Siri on Mac,” which is not the same as the broader Apple Intelligence section—this is a core entity mismatch (DEEP failure). Additionally, both agents reference the wrong macOS version (Sequoia/Sonoma instead of Tahoe), so neither fully satisfies the update-specific checklist, though A’s structure more closely matches the intended “What’s new” section framing.

On Width/Completeness, Agent A covers nearly all required sections, including password recovery (though not explicitly “recently deleted passkey” recovery). Agent B also omits that specific credential-recovery nuance and misses Apple Intelligence entirely. 

On Presentation & UX, both are well-structured with clear sectioning, but Agent A provides slightly richer descriptions and tighter alignment to the named support hubs. Citation density is also higher in A.

Therefore, Agent A wins for better entity accuracy and overall completeness, but not by a “much better” margin due to its macOS version inaccuracy and minor omissions.

rounds.question: A Mac user wants to begin using the system’s built‑in personal AI capabilities across their Apple devices and ensure their computer is properly maintained for compatibility. Based on Apple’s official platform guidance, explain: (1) the hardware models and minimum operating system versions required across Mac, iPhone, iPad, Apple Watch, and Apple Vision Pro; (2) the setup steps and device conditions necessary to activate the AI features (including storage, language, pairing, and download requirements); (3) notable regional or language limitations that may affect availability; and (4) the recommended built‑in macOS support actions for maintaining system readiness, including password recovery, operating system reinstallation, backups, and system updates. Provide a structured explanation that integrates requirements and maintenance considerations across all relevant devices.
checklist_width: Compatible Mac models (M1 or later) and macOS Sequoia 15.1 or later requirement
Compatible iPhone models (iPhone 15 Pro, iPhone 16 or later) and iOS 18.1 or later requirement
Compatible iPad models (M1 or later; iPad mini A17 Pro) and iPadOS 18.1 or later requirement
Compatible Apple Vision Pro and visionOS 2.4 or later requirement
Compatible Apple Watch models (Series 6 or later, Ultra models, SE 2 or later) with watchOS 11 or later and pairing requirement
Requirement to enable the feature under Settings > Apple Intelligence & Siri after updating software
7 GB on-device storage requirement (except Apple Watch)
Wi‑Fi and power connection recommended for on-device model download
Device language and Siri language must match supported languages
Regional restrictions (e.g., limitations in China mainland; EU restriction for Live Translation with AirPods)
Reset Apple ID password process as a support action
Reinstall macOS using macOS Recovery without deleting personal data
Back up Mac using Time Machine with external storage
Upgrade/update macOS to latest version for compatibility
checklist_depth: Apple Intelligence feature set + proof via hardware, OS version, storage, language, and regional eligibility logic
macOS Recovery + logic of reinstalling operating system without removing personal data
Time Machine + logic of external storage backup solution
Apple ID password reset + logic of account access recovery
macOS upgrade process + logic of maintaining latest compatible system version
rounds.rationale: The question uses contextual logic (getting started with a Mac, AI integration, and system maintenance) to indirectly point to Apple Intelligence and macOS support infrastructure without naming any source document. It requires deep reasoning to identify the correct feature set and associated devices (AI capability rollout across platforms) and wide aggregation of scattered requirements (hardware models, OS versions, storage, language, region limits) plus separate macOS maintenance procedures (password reset, reinstall, backup, update). The respondent must synthesize information across multiple platform support domains rather than relying on a single fact.
source_nodes: How to get Apple Intelligence - Apple Support
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: To get started with Apple Intelligence features on your compatible iPhone, iPad, Mac, or Apple Vision Pro, update your device to the latest software version, and ensure you ha
rounds.answer_a:
Apple Intelligence requires supported hardware plus recent OS versions across devices. On Mac, it works on Apple silicon models (MacBook Air/Pro, iMac, Mac mini, Mac Studio, Mac Pro with M1 or later) running macOS Sequoia 15.1 or later. On iPhone, supported models are iPhone 16 lineup, iPhone 15 Pro, and iPhone 15 Pro Max with iOS 18.1+. On iPad, a model with M1 or later or iPad mini (A17 Pro) is required with iPadOS 18.1+. Apple Watch support requires watchOS 11.1+, and Apple Vision Pro requires visionOS 2.4+ [1][2][3].

To turn it on, update each device, sign in to Apple Account, set Siri and device language to a supported language, and ensure enough free storage because Apple Intelligence models must download to the device. Apple says iPhone, iPad, and Mac must have at least 7 GB available. The feature generally requires Apple silicon-class processing, and some cross-device experiences depend on nearby paired devices using the same account and compatible software [1][2][4]. If Apple Intelligence is not enabled during setup, it can be turned on later in Siri/Apple Intelligence settings, after the on-device models finish downloading [1][4].

Availability is not universal. Features and language support vary by country/region and by feature. Apple publishes a current list of supported languages and notes that some capabilities may be unavailable or delayed in certain jurisdictions [1][5].

For system readiness on Mac, Apple’s built-in guidance is to keep macOS updated, back up regularly with Time Machine, know login-password recovery options, and reinstall macOS from Recovery if needed. Recovery also supports disk repair, restoring from backups, and reinstalling the operating system without separate media [6][7][8][9].

## References
[1] Apple Support, “Get started with Apple Intelligence on Mac.” https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/get-started-with-apple-intelligence-mh35738/mac  
[2] Apple Support, “Apple Intelligence requirements.” https://support.apple.com/121115  
[3] Apple Support, “Apple Intelligence is available in beta on all Apple Vision Pro models.” https://www.apple.com/newsroom/2025/02/apple-intelligence-is-available-in-beta-on-all-apple-vision-pro-models/  
[4] Apple Support, “Set up Apple Intelligence on iPhone.” https://support.apple.com/guide/iphone/set-up-apple-intelligence-iph00f208d05/ios  
[5] Apple Support, “Apple Intelligence feature availability.” https://www.apple.com/apple-intelligence/  
[6] Apple Support, “Update macOS on Mac.” https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/update-macos-mchlpx1065/mac  
[7] Apple Support, “Back up your files with Time Machine on Mac.” https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/back-up-files-with-time-machine-mh35860/mac  
[8] Apple Support, “If you forgot your Mac login password.” https://support.apple.com/102633  
[9] Apple Support, “Reinstall macOS.” https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/reinstall-macos-mchlp1599/mac
rounds.answer_b:
(1) Hardware & OS prerequisites  
• Mac – any model running macOS Sierra 10.12 or later can use Siri; hands-free “Hey Siri” requires MacBook Pro (2018 or later), MacBook Air (2018 or later), iMac (2020 with Apple T2 chip or later), iMac Pro, Mac mini (2020 with T2 or later), Mac Studio, or any Mac with Apple silicon, all on macOS Mojave 10.14 or later [1].  
• iPhone – iPhone 4s or newer with iOS 17 or current release for full on-device processing features [2].  
• iPad – iPad mini (5th gen), iPad (6th gen), iPad Air (3rd gen), iPad Pro (all models) on iPadOS 17+ [2].  
• Apple Watch – Apple Watch Series 3 or later with watchOS 10+; “Raise to speak” starts at Series 3 [3].  
• Apple Vision Pro – visionOS 1 (ships with device) brings voice-first Siri; no additional hardware requirement [4].

(2) Activation & device conditions  
• Connect to the internet (unless using offline commands supported in iOS/iPadOS 15+ and macOS Monterey+).  
• Turn on Siri in System Settings > Siri & Spotlight (Mac) or Settings > Siri & Search (iPhone/iPad/visionOS); on Apple Watch use Settings > Siri.  
• Ensure language/region is set to one of Siri’s supported languages; download any additional voice data (about 500 MB) when first enabled [1][2].  
• Enable “Listen for ‘Hey Siri’ ” (or “Siri”) and complete the five-phrase voice training; Mac notebooks must be plugged in the first time [1].  
• Pair devices with the same Apple ID to share Siri knowledge and Continuity features; at least 2 GB free storage recommended for voice models and logs [2].

(3) Regional/language limitations  
Siri is unavailable in Mainland China on Mac, Apple Watch, and Vision Pro; certain languages (e.g., Swedish, Dutch) lack full offline support or hands-free activation. Features like messaging, HomeKit, and Maps vary by country [2][5].

(4) macOS upkeep for compatibility  
• Password recovery – add Account Recovery contacts or Recovery Key in System Settings > Apple ID to regain access if locked out [6].  
• Backups – use Time Machine (automatic hourly snapshots) and store an external copy before major updates [7].  
• Reinstall macOS – start up in macOS Recovery (⌘-R) and choose Reinstall to refresh the system without erasing data [8].  
• System updates – enable automatic updates in System Settings > General > Software Update, including Rapid Security Responses, to ensure Siri language packs and on-device intelligence stay current [9].

## References  
[1] https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/use-siri-on-mac-mh15216/mac  
[2] https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT210515  
[3] https://support.apple.com/guide/watch/ask-siri-apd1a3f8f595/watchos  
[4] https://support.apple.com/guide/apple-vision-pro/ask-siri-devb5d8c6e0b/1.0/visionos  
[5] https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT204411  
[6] https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT202647  
[7] https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT201250  
[8] https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT204904  
[9] https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT201541
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies the topic as Apple Intelligence and outlines M1+ Macs, iOS/iPadOS 18.1+, visionOS 2.4+, storage (7 GB), language matching, on-device downloads, and macOS maintenance actions. Agent B misidentifies the entity entirely, focusing on legacy Siri requirements (e.g., macOS Sierra, iPhone 4s), which is not the built-in Apple Intelligence platform referenced in the query. This is a core entity failure.

Width/Completeness: Agent A covers most checklist items—hardware models (Mac M1+, iPhone 15 Pro/16+, iPad M1+/A17 Pro), OS minimums, storage requirements, language matching, enabling via Settings, regional variation, and macOS support actions (Time Machine, Recovery reinstall without data loss, password reset, updates). While it lacks some specifics (exact Apple Watch model list, explicit China/EU examples), it broadly satisfies the requirements. Agent B provides outdated and irrelevant compatibility details and misses Apple Intelligence-specific constraints entirely.

User Experience & Structure: Agent A is structured, concise, citation-backed, and aligned with the prompt’s four-part framework. Agent B is well formatted but fundamentally incorrect, making it unusable.

Conclusion: Because Agent B fails the core entity (DEEP failure) and Agent A substantially satisfies the checklist, Agent A is MUCH_BETTER.
